metadata.title: Suno vs 11 Labs Cost Test — ElevenLabs Voice
metadata.purpose: Video for 'Brutalist — Claude for Video Production' on @NikBearBrown. THE VIDEO IS THE EXPERIMENT: the same narration voiced through ElevenLabs (metered, generate_audio.py) and through Suno (flat, ./art suno → human generates with Bear's uploaded voice → vocal-only stem into pantry/ → ./art suno-slice). Teaches the second voice path: 4000-char .suno.N.txt export, the [spoken word] tag above every beat, the pantry drop, silence-detect slicing, and the same-interface principle (mp3/beat-<BID>.mp3 + measured durations — downstream never knows the engine). Real numbers from COST-LOG.md; the human picks the shipping voice. Basis doc: docs/suno-vs-11-labs-cost-test.md (authored from COST-LOG.md after the experiment).
metadata.clock: narration — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio (either engine)

beats.label: Announce — ElevenLabs version
beats.narration_text: This is the ElevenLabs version. The same video exists voiced by Suno — the link is in the description. You are the blind test.

Every video in this series speaks. And until today, every word of it cost ElevenLabs credits. This is the cost test: the same narration, voiced two different ways — and the video you're watching is the experiment.

production_viz.label: the itch — metered pricing
production_viz.mechanic: A tank labeled ELEVENLABS CREDITS draining as a character counter ticks up beat by beat: every beat, every retake, every regeneration draws from the same tank. A small bill line climbs in red. Caption: 'metered: the more you talk, the more you pay.'

beats.narration_text: Here's the itch. ElevenLabs is metered. Every character of every beat, every retake, every regeneration draws down the same tank. It's a great voice — but a series that talks a lot pays a lot.

production_viz.label: the challenger — flat generations
production_viz.mechanic: The Suno card: a voice waveform labeled 'your voice, uploaded once' and a flat-rate stamp: ONE GENERATION ≈ ONE VIDEO'S NARRATION (4000-character lyrics box). A small honest footnote in red: 'leans speak-song — clear for spoken word.' Caption: 'flat: the bill doesn't scale with the word count.'

beats.narration_text: The challenger is Suno. My voice lives there too — uploaded once. It leans a little speak-song, but for spoken word it's clear. And the pricing shape is different: not a meter, a flat generation — and one generation can carry an entire video's narration.

output: one file per 4000 characters — Suno's lyrics box.
no beat is ever split across files.

beats.narration_text: So the toolkit grew a second voice path. Art suno takes the same beat sheet — the single source of truth — and writes the narration out as text files, one per four thousand characters, because that's the size of Suno's lyrics box. No beat is ever split across a file boundary.

production_viz.label: the [spoken word] tag — above EVERY beat
production_viz.mechanic: A .suno.txt file mock: three beats of narration, each preceded by the [spoken word] tag in teal. To the side, a crossed-out version in red with the tag only at the top — and a musical note creeping into beat two. Caption: 'one style note is not enough; the tag rides above every beat.'

beats.narration_text: Every beat gets one meta tag above it: spoken word. Not once at the top — above every single beat. Because even when your only style note is spoken word, Suno will sometimes decide your sentence deserves a melody. The tag keeps it speaking.

production_viz.label: the human loop — three moves
production_viz.mechanic: Three-step flow: (1) paste .suno.N.txt into Suno's lyrics box, (2) generate with your voice + download the VOCAL-ONLY stem (a waveform with a crossed-out music bed), (3) drop it into pantry/ — the pantry folder drawn as the toolkit's human-media shelf. Caption: 'pantry/<slug>-vocals-N.wav — where human-made media always lands.'

beats.narration_text: The human loop is three moves. Paste a file into Suno. Generate with your voice and download the vocal-only stem — no music bed. Then drop it in the pantry. The pantry is where human-made media always lands in this toolkit.

output: segments ≠ beats → refuses to write. no quiet failures.

beats.narration_text: Then art suno-slice cuts the stem at the silences between beats — one mp3 per beat — and measures every one. If the segments don't match the beats, it refuses to touch anything and tells you which knob to turn. No quiet failures.

production_viz.label: the voice is a plug
production_viz.mechanic: Two engines — ELEVENLABS (metered) and SUNO (flat) — both feeding the SAME row of slots: mp3/beat-B00.mp3, beat-B01.mp3, …, each slot stamped with a measured duration. Below, the downstream pipeline (render · captions · shorts · publish) drawn as one unbroken teal line that never touches the engines. Caption: 'same files, same durations, same beat sheet — downstream never knows.'

beats.narration_text: And here's the part that matters. The slices land in exactly the slots ElevenLabs would have filled. Same files, same measured durations, same beat sheet. Downstream — the renders, the captions, the shorts, the publish — nothing knows which engine spoke. The voice is a plug.

production_viz.label: the numbers — from this build's COST-LOG
production_viz.mechanic: The cost table for THIS video, rendered from COST-LOG.md after the experiment: rows = engine (ElevenLabs metered / Suno flat), columns = credits or generations used, wall time, human steps, retakes, cost per finished narration minute. Real measured values only — the table is built from the log, never invented. Caption: 'metered scales with the word count; flat doesn't.'

beats.narration_text: The exact numbers from this build are on screen — what this video's narration cost through the meter, and what it cost flat. The shape of the answer was never in doubt: metered pricing scales with how much you talk. Flat pricing doesn't. A talkative series feels that difference on every single video.

production_viz.label: no free lunch
production_viz.mechanic: A two-pan balance. LEFT (ElevenLabs): 'steadier — take after take sounds the same' in teal. RIGHT (Suno): 'can drift musical' in red, with the workflow tax itemized underneath: retakes · vocal-only download · pantry drop · slicing. Caption: 'cheap has a workflow tax — the question is whether the tax is smaller than the bill.'

beats.narration_text: It isn't a free lunch. The metered voice is steadier — take after take sounds the same. The flat voice can drift musical, and you pay in retakes and slicing instead of credits. Cheap has a workflow tax. The question is whether the tax is smaller than the bill.

production_viz.label: the machine voices; you choose the voice
production_viz.mechanic: The hero beat — most care. Dark bg #2A1A0E. Center title in EB Garamond: 'THE MACHINE VOICES EVERY BEAT.' in white over 'YOU CHOOSE WHOSE VOICE SHIPS.' in teal. LEFT: both engine paths drawn mechanical and equal. RIGHT: a single human ear and a checkmark on one take. Caption: 'you listened to both — one of them is playing right now.'

beats.narration_text: So here's the split, one more time. The machine will voice every beat either way — meter or flat, it doesn't care. What it can't do is decide what you're willing to sound like. I listened to both takes of this very video. One of them is playing right now — because I picked it. You bring the taste.

output: every generation, every credit, every retake.
two engines · one narration · real numbers.
this video was built from this log.

beats.narration_text: And all of it — every generation, every credit, every retake — is in one honest log. Two engines, one narration, real numbers. This video was built from that log.
That's the cost test. Subscribe to follow the series, and find the whole toolkit at www dot brutalist dot art. You bring the taste; the machine brings the hands — and now, either voice.
